Exhibit 2-2: Fit Supply Chain Type to Product The diagram is divided into two main sections: Efficient Supply Chain: Positioned on the left side, this section is associated with low demand uncertainty. It contains two boxes labeled "Make-to-stock products." The top box corresponds to products with high volume, while the bottom box corresponds to products with low volume. Responsive Supply Chain: Positioned on the right side, this section is associated with high demand uncertainty.

It contains two boxes: The top box is labeled "Assemble-to-order products" and is associated with high volume. The bottom box is labeled "Make-to-order products" and is associated with low volume. The x-axis represents "Demand Uncertainty," ranging from low on the left to high on the right. The y-axis represents "Volume," ranging from low at the bottom to high at the top. The image categorizes different types of products based on their appropriate supply chain strategies considering the factors of volume and demand uncertainty. Go to image in text. Exhibit 2-7: Comprehensive Supply Chain Management System (Manufacturer’s Perspective) This image is a detailed diagram representing a comprehensive supply chain management system from the perspective of a manufacturer.

The diagram is divided into several vertical columns, each representing different entities within the supply chain: Suppliers’ Suppliers, Suppliers, Manufacturers, Distributors, Wholesalers, Retailers, and Customers. Top Arrows: The topmost part of the diagram features arrows indicating the flow of goods and services, information, and payments across the supply chain. The flow of goods and services moves from Suppliers’ Suppliers to Customers, while the flow of payments moves in the opposite direction, from Customers to Suppliers’ Suppliers. The information flow is bidirectional between these entities. Process Flow: The diagram details the process value supply chain and the value delivery supply chain, emphasizing the interconnectedness of various supply chain functions.

Systems Overview: ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning): Located centrally, the ERP system connects different entities like Suppliers, Manufacturers, Distributors, Wholesalers, and Retailers, ensuring the integration and flow of information. Sub-categories include Supplier ERP, Parent ERP, Distributor ERP, etc. APS (Advanced Planning and Scheduling): Below ERP, APS connects subsidiary ERP systems with plant-specific ERP systems, enhancing planning and scheduling functions across the supply chain. SCEM (Supply Chain Event Management): SCEM focuses on event visibility and active visibility across the supply chain, facilitating the management of unforeseen events and real-time updates.

WMS (Warehouse Management System): WMS systems manage inventory across different supply chain entities, from Suppliers’ Suppliers to Retailers. TMS (Transportation Management System): TMS coordinates transportation across the supply chain, including reverse logistics for returns or recycling. CRM (Customer Relationship Management) & SRM (Supplier Relationship Management): These systems manage relationships with customers and suppliers, facilitating communication and order management. Visual Elements: The diagram includes icons representing warehouses, transportation vehicles, and other supply chain elements, visually linking the different systems and their roles within the overall supply chain.

This comprehensive overview highlights the complex interactions and systems involved in managing a modern supply chain from the manufacturer’s viewpoint. Go to image in text. Exhibit 2-8: ERP System Functionality This image is a circular diagram representing the functionalities of an ERP system. At the center of the circle is a blue circle labeled "Shared Master Data ERP Database," which serves as the core of the system. Radiating out from the central ERP Database, the diagram is divided into various segments, each representing different business functions: Sales: Includes functionalities like sales orders, pricing, billing, quotes, and available-to-promise (ATP) calculations. Finance: Covers general ledger, assets, accounts payable (A/P), and accounts receivable (A/R).

Human Resources: Encompasses personnel management, travel, payroll, and time management. Purchasing: Manages purchase orders (POs), contracts, quotas, requirements, and vendor lists. Manufacturing: Handles shop floor control, production orders, and repetitive manufacturing processes. Planning: Focuses on S&OP, MPS/MRP, bill of materials, and scheduling. Logistics: Involves quality management, receiving, issuing, warehousing, and inventory control. Supply Chain Event Management, Transportation Management System (TMS), and Warehouse Management System (WMS): Supports logistics and supply chain activities. Advanced Planning and Scheduling, Engineering Change Control: Provides tools for complex planning and managing changes in engineering.

Customer Relationship Management (CRM) and Supplier Relationship Management (SRM): Connects the ERP system to customer and supplier interactions. The diagram visually communicates how all these business functions are interconnected through the central ERP database, highlighting the integrated nature of an ERP system and its importance in managing various aspects of an organization. Go to image in text. Exhibit 2-9: Advanced Planning and Scheduling Systems The image depicts an APS system and its integration with various ERP systems. In the center of the image, there is a circle labeled "ERP Headquarters," which represents the central ERP system of a company.

Surrounding the ERP Headquarters are two smaller circles labeled "ERP Plant 1" and "ERP Plant 2," representing the ERP systems at different manufacturing plants. These plants are directly connected to the ERP Headquarters, indicating data flow and integration between them. The entire system, including ERP Headquarters and the two plant ERPs, is enclosed within a blue oval labeled "APS System." This suggests that the APS system oversees and integrates the planning and scheduling activities across the different ERP systems within the company. Outside the APS system, on the left, there is a set of databases labeled "Component Manufacturers’ ERP," representing the ERP systems used by external suppliers or component manufacturers.

On the right, another set of databases is labeled "Distributors’ ERP," indicating the ERP systems used by the company’s distributors. Arrows indicate the data flow and communication between these various ERP systems, showing how the APS system enables coordination between the headquarters, manufacturing plants, suppliers, and distributors. This integration ensures that all parts of the supply chain are synchronized and can respond effectively to planning and scheduling needs. Go to image in text. Exhibit 2-10: Advanced Planning and Scheduling System Modules The image illustrates the functionality of an APS system and its integration with ERP systems.

The diagram is circular, showing the continuous and interconnected processes managed by the APS system: At the center of the circle is a small, central circle labeled "APS," representing the Advanced Planning and Scheduling system. Surrounding the APS circle are four main processes arranged in a clockwise flow: Demand Management, Resource Management, Requirements Optimization, and Resource Allocation Above the circle, two ERP systems are depicted as cylindrical databases labeled "ERP." These represent the company’s ERP systems that integrate with the APS system. Arrows flow between the ERP systems and the APS modules, indicating the continuous data exchange and process synchronization between the ERP systems and the APS system for effective planning, scheduling, and execution.

This circular design highlights the interconnectedness of these APS modules and their continuous interaction with ERP systems to ensure optimal planning and scheduling within an organization. Go to image in text. Exhibit 2-12: Information System Architecture Components The image illustrates the key components of an Information System Architecture and how they are interconnected. The architecture is presented in a hierarchical manner: LAN/WAN Network: A central horizontal line labeled "LAN/WAN" represents the local area network (LAN) or wide area network (WAN) that serves as the backbone of the system. Clients (Wireless Devices): On the left, above the LAN/WAN line, are various wireless client devices, including a laptop, tablet, and smartphone.

These devices connect wirelessly to the LAN/WAN. Clients (Peripheral): In the center above the LAN/WAN line, a printer is depicted as a peripheral client connected to the LAN/WAN network, likely representing a wired or networked printer. Clients (Wired Connection): On the right, above the LAN/WAN line, a laptop is shown connected via a wired connection to the LAN/WAN. Server Components: Below the LAN/WAN line, there are several server components: Server (DBMS): A database management server (DBMS) is shown connected to a database, indicating the role of this server in managing and storing data. Server: Additional servers are shown connected to the LAN/WAN, representing various server functionalities within the system.

Internet and Cloud Computing: On the left, connected to the LAN/WAN network, there is a label for "Internet (configuration choice)" with an arrow pointing to the internet/cloud. This suggests an optional internet-based configuration for the system. Below the internet, there is a "Cloud Computing (configuration choice)" section. This includes servers and databases within a cloud environment, indicating an alternative or complementary setup for hosting and managing resources in the cloud. Flow and Connectivity: Arrows represent the flow of data and connectivity between the various components, including client devices, servers, databases, and cloud services.

The image emphasizes the flexibility of configuration choices, including local LAN/WAN setups, internet-based configurations, and cloud computing options. Go to image in text. Exhibit 2-13: Organizational and Information System Architectures The image presents a detailed comparison between Organizational Architecture and Information System Architecture, arranged side by side to show their alignment and interaction. It is structured into four major sections, each linking an organizational aspect to its corresponding IT counterpart. Top Section - Organizational Strategy vs.

Information Strategy: Organizational Strategy: This section outlines the mission, vision, and core competencies of the organization, focusing on the development of an efficient, effective, and flexible extended supply chain strategy. It includes: Mission, vision, strategy Core competencies Segmentation Extended supply chain strategy Information Strategy: On the IT side, this section defines the principles, goals, and strategic vision for the information system, emphasizing efficiency, responsiveness, and customer focus. It includes: Commitment, principles, goals Priorities: efficient, responsive, customer-focused Vision of information system structure Gap analysis Second Section - Information Content Definition vs.

Information Policies and Controls: Information Content Definition: This part details how information is managed within the organization, including data entry, storage, access, and the strategic model of technology integration. It includes: Data entry, accuracy, and storage Extended supply chain business model Distribution strategy (logistics and integration) Information Policies and Controls: On the IT side, it focuses on policies, controls, and governance standards needed to ensure data integrity and effective communication within the extended supply chain. It includes: Specific policies and controls, governance methods Required level of networking and communication Third Section - Information Infrastructure Design vs.

Database, Networks, Software, Configuration: Information Infrastructure Design: This section covers the design of the organizational infrastructure, including database design, networking, software, and process integration in the extended supply chain. It includes: Database design Networking Configuration (e.g., hardware, client/server) Extended supply chain process model Database, Networks, Software, Configuration: On the IT side, it provides details on the specific IT infrastructure, including databases, networking specifics, software, and system integration. It includes: Specific database/cloud choices Networking specifics (e.g., data communications) Configuration specifics (e.g., interfaces) Bottom Section - Information Infrastructure Change vs.

Action Plan, Schedule, Prioritization: Information Infrastructure Change: This section addresses the requirements for IT change within the organizational infrastructure and the extended supply chain IT requirements. It includes: IT change requirements Extended supply chain IT requirements Action Plan, Schedule, Prioritization: On the IT side, it aligns with the development of an action plan, schedule, and prioritization of IT changes. It includes: IT action plan Extended supply chain IT action plan This image illustrates how organizational strategy and processes are directly connected to the design and implementation of information systems, ensuring alignment between business goals and IT capabilities within an extended supply chain. 4o Go to image in text.

ABC Company internet site has an arrow pointing towards the Manufacturer.

Supply Chain Flow: Below the internet sites, the supply chain is depicted as a sequence of steps, labeled from 1 to 7: Tier 2 Supplier Raw Material Supplier Manufacturer Distributor Wholesaler Retailer End User Process Flow: Arrows indicate the direction of material flow from the raw material supplier to the end user, passing through various stages such as manufacturing, distribution, and retailing. Conversely, arrows representing "Payments and returns" flow from the end user back up the supply chain, reflecting the financial transactions and any returns that may occur.

Cloud and Internet: The connections between the internet sites (XYZ and ABC companies) and the respective suppliers (raw material supplier and manufacturer) are represented by arrows emanating from the cloud symbol, denoting the use of online platforms to manage supply chain activities. Go to image in text. Exhibit 2-16: Adaptive Sell-Side E-Commerce Website The image details an Adaptive Sell-Side E-Commerce Website process, depicting the flow of activities that occur when a user interacts with an e-commerce platform, specifically the ABC Company internet site. Top Left Corner: The process begins with an internet user accessing the web through their device, which is connected to the internet.

The user reaches the ABC Company internet site, hosted on a server that is connected to the World Wide Web. The interaction starts from the left side, where the user is guided through a sequence of steps to complete their online transaction. Steps 1 to 8: Step 1: Attract the user Step 2: Provide information Step 3: Select/customize choices Step 4: Perform transaction Step 5: Pay Step 6: Follow-up capabilities Step 7: Deliver the products Step 8: Personalize the user’s buying preferences Visual Flow: The steps are laid out in a sequential manner with arrows indicating the flow from one step to the next. The process starts with attracting the user and moves through providing information, customizing choices, performing the transaction, and making payment.

After the payment is made, the focus shifts to delivery, follow-up, and personalization of the user’s preferences for future transactions. Go to image in text. Exhibit 2-17: VMI and Consignment Combinations The image presents a matrix that explains different scenarios involving Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI) and consignment inventory. The matrix is divided into four quadrants, with the vertical axis labeled "Consignment?" (Yes/No) and the horizontal axis labeled "VMI?" (Yes/No). Each quadrant describes a different combination of VMI and consignment practices: Top Left (No Consignment, Yes VMI): "Supplier decides on replenishment. Replenishment goods are immediately invoiced.

Buyer owns inventory." Top Right (No Consignment, No VMI): "Traditional: Organization owns and manages inventory or sells it to independent distributors who order and manage their own inventory." Bottom Left (Yes Consignment, Yes VMI): "Supplier decides on replenishment, but only sold inventory is invoiced. Supplier employs restockers, e.g., Frito Lay pays restockers per bag of chips sold to promote proactive restocking." Bottom Right (Yes Consignment, No VMI): "Seller wants/needs items on-site but may not be fast-selling, e.g., hospital controls stock of pacemakers owned by supplier. When one is used, one is sent to replenish inventory and invoice is sent for the used one." Go to image in text.

Exhibit 2-24: Decision Support Systems (DSS) The process is as follows: Management formulates a business scenario and requests the DSS system to evaluate and analyze it. DSS software searches for the appropriate information in the ERP system, formats it, and applies logic to it. DSS software analyzes the selected data and provides management with alternative courses of action based on the requested scenario. The flowchart shows the direction of information flow from management to DSS software, then to the ERP system, and back to the DSS software, which finally presents the information back to management through a laptop representing the presentation of information. Go to image in text.

Exhibit 2-26: Custom Scorecard This image represents a custom scorecard for a Monthly Quality of Service (QOS) Report at a 3PL (third-party logistics) site labeled as "Site XXX." The scorecard is divided into several categories: Active Suppliers & Parts, ASN Compliance, Receipts, Receipt Discrepancies, Inventory Count, Deliveries, and Corrective Actions. Each category has specific metrics tracked, such as the number of active suppliers and parts, percentage of shipments received without ASN, number of containers received, percentage of shipments received late in the system, and others.

The data is presented in a table format, with the first column listing the metrics, followed by a "Target" column that sets expected performance levels (some are blank), and subsequent columns representing each month from January to December. The table is color-coded in light pink for category headers. Monthly data are filled in with numbers or percentages, indicating the site’s performance on each metric. The data shows varying levels of performance throughout the year, with some metrics, such as shipments received without ASN and inventory accuracy, showing fluctuations across months. This scorecard provides a detailed overview of the site’s operational performance, helping identify areas that need improvement. Go to image in text.

Exhibit 2-27: Visual Tools for Scorecards This image presents six bar graphs, each illustrating a specific performance metric over the course of a year, from January (J) to December (D). The metrics being tracked include: # Freight Damages Found at Receipt: This graph tracks the number of freight damages found at receipt each month. The bars show a gradual increase, with the number of damages ranging from around 6 to 10 by December. % of Shipments Received without ASN: This graph displays the percentage of shipments received without an Advanced Shipping Notice (ASN) from two suppliers (Supplier A and Supplier B). Supplier A starts with 100% in January, decreasing over the months, while Supplier B starts lower and also shows a decreasing trend.

# Corrective Action Reports Filed: This graph shows the number of corrective action reports filed each month, with the highest number reaching 2 or 3 in a few months. Inventory Accuracy: This graph tracks inventory accuracy each month, with the percentage mostly staying above 75%, showing fluctuations but generally maintaining a high level of accuracy. % Shipments Delivered Late to Factory: This graph shows the percentage of shipments delivered late to the factory each month, which remains at 0% for most months, with slight increases in certain months, up to around 15%. # Shipments with Quality Discrepancies: This graph records the number of shipments with quality discrepancies each month, with the number varying, peaking at around 2 or 3 in certain months.

These visual tools are used in scorecards to track and analyze the performance of suppliers and logistics operations over time, providing a clear and immediate view of trends and areas that may require attention or improvement. Direct Materials Cost = Quantity Purchased x Unit Cost Direct Materials Used = Quantity Used x Unit Cost Direct Labor Cost = Standard Hours x Hourly Rate Overhead Cost = Cost Driver x (Total Overhead / Total Cost Driver) Go to image in text. Exhibit 2-42: Sample Balance Sheet Showing Two Years of Results Key Concepts: Balance Sheets: Statement of financial value at a point in time (end of year). Assets: What the organization owns. Current Assets: Assets expected to be converted to cash within one year. Cash and Cash Equivalents Inventory Accounts Receivable Total Current Assets Fixed Assets: Long-term assets not easily converted to cash.

Gross Property, Plant and Equipment Less: Accumulated Depreciation New Property, Plant, and Equipment Total Assets Liabilities: Amounts owed to others. Current Liabilities: Amounts owed this year. Accounts Payable Short-Term Notes Payable Total Current Liabilities: Amounts owed beyond one year. Long Term Liabilities Long-Term Debt Total Liabilities Owners’ Equity: Funds from owners and operations (what is left after liabilities are deducted). Common Stock (Par Value) and Additional Paid-In Capital: What owners have contributed. Retained Earnings: Reinvested funds from operations. Total Owners’ Equity Total Liabilities and Owner’s Equity Equation: Assets = Liabilities + Owners’ Equity. Go to image in text.

Exhibit 2-43: Sample Income Statement Showing Two Years of Results Key Concepts: Income Statements For the Years Ending: Profit or loss over a period of time. Revenue (Sales): Direct Labor, Direct Materials, and Factory Overhead: Expenses from providing goods/services that generate revenue. Less: COGS: Cost of Goods Sold, calculated as Direct Labor + Direct Materials + Factory Overhead. Gross Profit: Revenue - COGS. Less: Operating Expenses: General expenses from running the business that cannot be directly linked to specific units of goods/services sold, including Selling Expenses, General and Administrative, and Lease Expense. Less: Depreciation: Lowers fixed asset value for taxes. Less: Interest Expense: Payments on debt.

Net Income (Profit) Before Taxes: Shows effect of taxes on profits, calculated as Gross Profit - Total Operating Expenses - Depreciation - Interest Expense. Net Income (Profit): Net Income Before Taxes - Income Taxes, representing the "bottom line" profit of the company. Net Income Per Share-Basic: Net Income divided by the number of outstanding shares. Go to image in text. Exhibit 2-44: Sample Statement of Cash Flows Showing Two Years of Results Key Concepts: Cash Flow Statement: A report that shows the change in a company’s cash balance over a period of time. Operating Section: A viable firm needs positive cash flow from operations in most years. After-Tax Net Income Depreciation Add-Back: Depreciation is deducted on the income statement but doesn’t reduce cash (added back).

(Increase)/Decrease in Inventory: Increase in inventory or accounts receivable reduces cash. (Increase)/Decrease in Accounts Receivable: Increase in inventory or accounts receivable reduces cash. Increase/(Decrease) in Accounts Payable: Increase in accounts payable increases cash. Cash Flow from Operations: Total cash generated from operating activities. Investing Section: Capex Spend (Capital Expenditures): Increase in business investments decreases cash. Cash Flow from Operations and Investment: Combined cash flow from operations and investing activities. Financing Section: Additional Equity Capital: Increase in new debt or equity provides cash.

Less Dividends Paid Increase/(Decrease) in Long-Term Debt Increase/(Decrease) in Short-Term Notes Cash Flow from Operations, Investments, and Financing: Combined cash flow from all sections. Cash Balances: Beginning Cash Balance Ending Cash Balance: Net Income +/– Change in Operating +/– Change in Investing +/– Change in Financing + Beginning Cash = Ending Cash. Go to image in text. Exhibit 2-47: Strategic Profit Model with Sample Data The Strategic Profit Model (SPM) illustrated in the image presents a step-by-step calculation of Return on Assets (ROA) using provided sample data. The image is structured with formulas and calculations, where different components such as Net Profit Margin and Asset Turnover are combined to compute ROA.

Key components include: Net Profit Margin: Calculated as Net Profit divided by Net Sales, with a resulting value of 0.060 (6%). Asset Turnover: Calculated as Net Sales divided by Total Assets, yielding a value of 2.38. Return on Assets (ROA): The product of Net Profit Margin and Asset Turnover, resulting in an ROA of 0.143 (14.3%). The image also breaks down each major component: Net Profit: The difference between Gross Profit (USD 200) and Total Expenses (USD 140), equaling USD 60. Gross Profit: Calculated as Net Sales (USD 1,000) minus the Cost of Goods Sold (USD 800). Total Assets: The sum of Current Assets (USD 280) and Fixed Assets (USD 140), totaling USD 420. Current Assets: Include Inventory (USD 180), Accounts Receivable (USD 40), and Other Current Assets (USD 60).

The model uses color coding to distinguish between input cells and calculated values, providing a clear and organized view of the financial calculations within the strategic profit model. Exhibit 2-48: Strategic Profit Model with Reduction in Inventory, Carrying Cost, and Net Sales This Strategic Profit Model (SPM) visualizes the calculation of Return on Assets (ROA) after implementing reductions in inventory, carrying costs, and net sales. The model breaks down various financial components to show how changes in operational efficiencies impact overall profitability. Key calculations and components include: Return on Assets (ROA): Calculated by multiplying the Net Profit Margin (0.061 or 6.1%) by Asset Turnover (2.61), resulting in an ROA of 0.158 (15.8%).

Net Profit Margin: Defined as Net Profit divided by Net Sales, showing the profitability per unit of sales. The Net Profit is USD 60, derived from Gross Profit (USD 190) minus Total Expenses (USD 130). Asset Turnover: Represents how efficiently a company uses its assets to generate sales, calculated as Net Sales (USD 990) divided by Total Assets (USD 380), resulting in a turnover of 2.61. Gross Profit: The difference between Net Sales (USD 990) and Cost of Goods Sold (USD 800), showing the profitability before subtracting operating expenses. Total Expenses: Comprising Variable Expenses (USD 70) and Fixed Expenses (USD 60), which are deducted from Gross Profit to determine Net Profit.

Total Assets: The sum of Current Assets (USD 240) and Fixed Assets (USD 140), with Current Assets including Inventory (USD 140), Accounts Receivable (USD 40), and Other Current Assets (USD 60). This model uses color coding to distinguish between input cells and calculated values, helping visualize the effects of reduced inventory and costs on the overall financial performance.
